Group Policy & Security Hardening
OUs · Security Groups · GPOs · Endpoint Hardening █
// about

Building out a realistic OU and group structure on top of the jflaum.cyber domain, then using Group Policy to enforce least privilege and harden every domain-joined machine — password and lockout policy, logon-hour restrictions, screen lock, Defender AV, the firewall, AppLocker, USB blocking, audit logging, and local security hardening.

GPMCActive Directory Users and ComputersWindows DefenderAppLockerRBAC
// ou & security group design

Planning the Structure design

Before touching the console, mapped out the OU hierarchy, the GPOs that would link to each OU, the security groups behind role-based access, and a simple 3-tier least-privilege model.

OU structure, linked GPOs, security groups, and access tiers for jflaum.cyber
  • OUs: Exec, MiddleManagement, Sales, IT, Security, HR, Finance, Servers, Workstations — each with its own Computers sub-OU
  • Tier 1 (Exec): broad read access, restricted write
  • Tier 2 (IT / Security): admin rights, scoped to role
  • Tier 3 (everyone else): standard user, time-restricted logon

Building Out OUs, Users & Groups

Created the OU tree in Active Directory Users and Computers, populated each with representative users, and created a matching security group per OU for GPO targeting and RBAC.

creating the CEO user object inside the Exec OU
creating a Finance user — the OU tree (Exec, MiddleManagement, Sales, IT, Security, HR, Finance, Servers) fully built out on the left
creating GRP_Exec as a global security group inside the Exec OU
adding IT Admin1 to GRP_ITAdmins
adding the IT admin group to Domain Admins
GRP_ExecGRP_ITAdminsGRP_StandardUsersGRP_SecOps
// password & account lockout policy

GPO_PasswordPolicy

Created and linked a domain-wide password and lockout policy through GPMC.

creating GPO_PasswordPolicy in Group Policy Management
password policy settings applied
SettingValue
Minimum password length8 characters
Maximum password age180 days
Minimum password age1 day
Password history24 remembered
Complexity requirementsEnabled
account lockout policy — 5 failed attempts, 30 minute lockout/reset, admin lockout enabled
8+ char minimum24 password history5-attempt lockout
// logon hours & screen lock

GPO_LogonHours

Restricted standard users to business hours and forced a logoff once those hours expire — applied via a PowerShell script against the standard users group rather than the GUI, since per-user logon-hour bitmasks aren't exposed in ADUC directly.

PowerShell script applying Mon–Fri 7am–6pm logon hours to every member of GRP_StandardUsers
"Force logoff when logon hours expire" enabled in GPO_LogonHours
# Apply Mon-Fri 7am-6pm logon hours to all members of GRP_StandardUsers $group = Get-ADGroupMember -Identity "GRP_StandardUsers" -Recursive $hours = [byte[]](0,0,0,0,0,224,255,3,224,255,3,224,255,3,224,255,3,224,0) foreach ($user in $group) { Set-ADUser -Identity $user.SamAccountName -LogonHours $hours }

GPO_ScreenLock

Forced an idle screen lock domain-wide so unattended workstations don't sit unlocked.

  • Enabled the screen saver and set Password protect the screen saver
  • Set the idle timeout to 900 seconds (15 minutes)
enabling the screen saver policy
setting the screen saver timeout to 900 seconds
linking GPO_RestrictControlPanel — full GPO list visible: LogonHours, PasswordPolicy, RestrictControlPanel, ScreenLock
15 min idle lockForced logoff
// endpoint security

GPO_DefenderAV

Locked Microsoft Defender Antivirus on for every domain-joined machine and tuned its cloud-protection settings.

creating GPO_DefenderAV
"Turn off Microsoft Defender Antivirus" set to Disabled — Defender can't be switched off on clients
Join Microsoft MAPS set to Advanced membership for cloud-delivered protection
Block at First Sight enabled for real-time cloud lookups on new files
scheduled full scan set to run weekly on Saturday
  • Also enabled: scan removable drives, scan all downloaded files/attachments, behavior monitoring, and email scanning
  • Sample submission set to send safe samples automatically for faster signature updates

Windows Defender Firewall

Enforced the firewall across all three network profiles rather than leaving it on defaults.

Domain Profile — firewall on, inbound blocked by default, outbound allowed
Private Profile configured the same way
  • Public Profile locked down identically
  • Disabled merging of local firewall/connection security rules so only domain-pushed rules apply

GPO_AppLocker & GPO_NoUSB

Layered application allow-listing with AppLocker and blocked removable storage on the more sensitive OUs (Exec, Finance, HR, MiddleManagement, Sales, Security).

configuring AppLocker rule enforcement in GPO_AppLocker
GPO_NoUSB linked to the Exec OU before creating GPO_AppLocker
creating GPO_NoUSB — full OU tree visible with GPO_RestrictControlPanel already linked everywhere
linking GPO_NoUSB — confirming the complete GPO inventory for the domain
"Scan removable drives" enabled so any USB device that does get connected is scanned
AppLockerRemovable storage blockedExec / Finance / HR / Sales / Security

GPO_RestrictControlPanel

Prevented standard users in non-IT OUs from reaching Control Panel or PC settings, cutting down on accidental misconfiguration and shadow-IT changes.

"Prohibit access to Control Panel and PC settings" enabled
// audit policy

GPO_AuditPolicy

Turned on advanced audit policy logging so logon activity, privilege use, and policy changes actually show up in the Security event log instead of going unrecorded.

creating GPO_AuditPolicy
Audit Logon — Success and Failure
Audit Sensitive Privilege Use — Failure
Audit Audit Policy Change — Success and Failure, so changes to the audit policy itself are logged
Advanced Audit PolicyLogon EventsPrivilege UsePolicy Change
// security hardening

GPO_SecurityHardening

Cleaned up a handful of well-known default-account and login-banner weaknesses.

creating GPO_SecurityHardening — full GPO list visible (AuditPolicy, DefenderAV, LogonHours, PasswordPolicy, ScreenLock)
renaming the built-in Administrator account to jflaum_admin to throw off automated attacks targeting the default name
disabling the Guest account
setting the interactive logon banner to "Authorized Users Only"
Admin RenameGuest DisabledLogon Banner
// verification

Confirming Policy Applied

Forced a policy refresh and pulled both the text and HTML versions of the resultant set of policy (RSoP) to confirm everything landed.

gpupdate /force on DC01
gpresult /r — Applied Group Policy Objects: GPO_PasswordPolicy, GPO_LogonHours, GPO_AuditPolicy
HTML Group Policy Results report — all components reporting Success, no errors detected
# Verify applied GPOs on any domain-joined machine gpresult /r gpresult /h report.html
// what i learned
  • How to design an OU/group structure around least privilege before building it, instead of bolting policy on after the fact
  • Per-user logon-hour restrictions aren't exposed cleanly in the ADUC GUI — bulk-applying them needs PowerShell
  • How GPO link order and scope (domain vs. OU) determine which policy wins when settings overlap
  • Why audit policy needs to be explicitly configured — Windows doesn't log most security-relevant events out of the box
  • How to verify policy actually applied with gpresult instead of assuming the GPMC console reflects reality on the client
// next steps
  • Re-run the Nessus scan against the hardened client and compare findings before/after
  • Add a WMI filter so GPO_NoUSB only targets workstations, not servers
  • Build out a Fine-Grained Password Policy for the Exec OU specifically
  • Forward Security event log audit data somewhere centralized for review
